title: Operating Systems Overview and Purpose
obj: Students will be able to describe the purpose of an operating system, identify the major OS families, and explain the role of the kernel, file system, and user interface.
content: manages hardware resources; provides services to applications; isolates processes from each other
core OS code running in privileged mode; hardware abstraction layer; system calls
Windows, macOS, Linux distributions, ChromeOS, iOS/Android — market share and use cases
organizes files into directories; tracks storage allocation; metadata (permissions, timestamps)
CLI vs. GUI; desktop environments (Windows Explorer, GNOME, Aqua); shell as a text interface
how a hypervisor lets multiple OS instances share one hardware platform
assess: Comparison table: list Windows, macOS, and a Linux distro side-by-side on license, default shell, and primary use case
Short answer: what would happen to running applications if the kernel crashed?
Discussion: why might a company run Linux on its servers but Windows on its desktops?

title: File Management and File Systems
obj: Students will be able to navigate a file system, create and organize files and folders, and describe how common file systems handle storage and permissions.
content: directories and paths (absolute vs. relative); root directory; file extensions and types
NTFS (permissions, journaling, encryption), FAT32 (legacy, USB), exFAT (cross-platform large files)
ext4 (Linux default), APFS (macOS), HFS+ (legacy); case sensitivity differences
copy, move, rename, delete, search; recycle bin vs. permanent deletion; hidden files
owner/group/other on Linux; NTFS ACLs on Windows; why permissions matter
naming conventions, folder hierarchies, archiving vs. deleting old files
assess: Navigation lab: using only the CLI, create a folder structure with 3 levels and move files between them
Permissions exercise: on a Linux VM, set a file so only the owner can read/write it
Short answer: why is NTFS preferred over FAT32 for a Windows system drive?

title: Microsoft Windows Editions
obj: Students will be able to differentiate Windows Home, Pro, Enterprise, and Education editions by feature set and identify the appropriate edition for a given scenario.
content: Home, Pro, Pro for Workstations, Enterprise, Education — licensing model differences
BitLocker, Remote Desktop host, domain join, Group Policy, Hyper-V — only in Pro+
volume licensing, Windows Update for Business, LTSC channel, extra security features
XP → Vista → 7 → 8/8.1 → 10 → 11; support lifecycle; why old versions are a risk
Home to Pro upgrade; in-place upgrade vs. clean install; activation and product keys
OEM tied to hardware; retail transferable; how to check activation status
assess: Edition matching: given 5 workplace scenarios, identify the correct Windows edition and justify
Short answer: a school wants Group Policy management — which edition do they need?
Activation check: on a lab machine, find and record the Windows edition, build number, and activation status

title: Windows Features and System Tools
obj: Students will be able to use built-in Windows administrative tools to view system information, manage tasks, configure settings, and access help resources.
content: processes, performance graphs, startup items, services tab; killing hung processes
Computer Management, Device Manager, Disk Management, Event Viewer, Local Users and Groups
hardware summary, components, software environment; useful for support calls
legacy vs. modern UI; which settings still live in Control Panel; how to open both
detailed CPU/disk/network/memory usage; creating baselines; identifying bottlenecks
checking update status, cumulative vs. feature updates, pausing updates, update troubleshooting
assess: Lab: open Task Manager and identify the top 3 CPU-consuming processes; end a non-responsive process
Tool scavenger hunt: use msinfo32, Device Manager, and Event Viewer to complete a system info worksheet
Short answer: what is the difference between the Services tab in Task Manager and the Services MMC snap-in?

title: Windows Command Line and PowerShell
obj: Students will be able to navigate the file system, manage files, and run common administrative commands using both CMD and PowerShell.
content: cd, dir, copy, move, del, mkdir, rmdir; absolute vs. relative paths; wildcards
ping, ipconfig, netstat, tracert, nslookup — reading the output
sfc /scannow, chkdsk, diskpart, taskkill, shutdown; running as administrator
cmdlet syntax (Verb-Noun), pipeline, Get-Help, Get-Command; why PS is preferred over CMD
Get-Process, Stop-Process, Get-Service, Start-Service, Get-EventLog; real admin use cases
.bat and .ps1 scripts; running scripts with Set-ExecutionPolicy; automating repetitive tasks
assess: CMD lab: navigate to a folder, create files, list contents, and delete — using only keyboard commands
PowerShell task: write a one-liner that lists all running services and outputs to a text file
Short answer: why would a tech use PowerShell instead of CMD for a complex administrative task?

title: Configuring Windows Settings
obj: Students will be able to locate and configure common Windows settings including display, power, time/region, accessibility, and privacy settings.
content: resolution, scale, orientation, multi-monitor arrangement, night light, refresh rate
Balanced, High Performance, Power Saver; sleep vs. hibernate; lid-close behavior on laptops
time zone, date format, language packs, keyboard layout; NTP sync (w32tm)
Magnifier, Narrator, high contrast, sticky keys, on-screen keyboard
diagnostic data levels, app permissions (camera/mic/location), activity history
local vs. Microsoft account; PIN setup; account type (Standard vs. Administrator)
assess: Settings walkthrough: configure a provided checklist of settings on a lab PC (display scale, power plan, region)
Scenario: a user's laptop is sleeping too aggressively during presentations — identify and fix the setting
Short answer: what is the difference between Sleep and Hibernate power states?

title: Windows Client Networking Features
obj: Students will be able to configure and verify Windows network adapter settings, share resources, and use Windows networking tools to diagnose connectivity.
content: IPv4/IPv6 settings, DNS, gateway; enabling/disabling adapters; adapter priority
Public, Private, Domain — firewall rules tied to each; changing profile type
inbound/outbound rules; allowing an app through; advanced settings; resetting defaults
enabling sharing, setting share permissions, accessing UNC paths (\\server\share)
Workgroup vs. domain; Network and Sharing Center; mapping network drives
built-in Windows VPN; L2TP, SSTP, IKEv2 protocols; connecting and verifying
assess: Lab: configure a static IP, verify it in ipconfig, ping the gateway, then revert to DHCP
Sharing lab: create a shared folder, set permissions, and access it from another VM via UNC path
Short answer: why should a laptop use the Public profile on a coffee shop Wi-Fi?

title: macOS Features and Tools
obj: Students will be able to navigate macOS, identify its key features and built-in utilities, and describe how it differs from Windows in common support scenarios.
content: Finder, Dock, Menu Bar, Spotlight, Mission Control; window management gestures
key panes: Displays, Sound, Network, Users & Groups, Security & Privacy, Software Update
Terminal (bash/zsh), Activity Monitor, Disk Utility, Console, Network Utility, Keychain Access
APFS volume structure; /Users /Applications /Library; case sensitivity; .DS_Store files
Safe Mode, Recovery Mode (Cmd+R), Internet Recovery, First Aid in Disk Utility; SIP
different driver model, App Store/DMG installs, Gatekeeper, iCloud integration
assess: Navigation task: use Spotlight to open Activity Monitor and identify the top memory-consuming process
Short answer: a macOS app won't open and shows 'unidentified developer' — what setting controls this and how do you bypass it?
Comparison: list three macOS equivalents to Windows tools (e.g., Activity Monitor = Task Manager)

title: Linux Client Features and Tools
obj: Students will be able to navigate a Linux desktop environment, use the terminal for common tasks, and identify how Linux differs from Windows and macOS in support scenarios.
content: Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS/RHEL, Mint; desktop environments (GNOME, KDE, XFCE)
ls, pwd, cd, cp, mv, rm, mkdir, chmod, chown, sudo, man; case-sensitive commands
apt/apt-get (Debian/Ubuntu), dnf/yum (RHEL/Fedora); installing, updating, removing software
rwx bits, numeric notation (755, 644), sudo vs root, /etc/passwd and /etc/sudoers
viewing logs (/var/log), checking processes (ps aux, top), managing services (systemctl)
assess: Terminal lab: complete a task sheet using only CLI (create user, install a package, set permissions)
Log analysis: given a /var/log/syslog excerpt, identify the error and its likely cause
Short answer: what command would you use to give a script execute permission for all users?

title: OS Installation and Upgrade Procedures
obj: Students will be able to perform a clean OS installation, configure initial settings, and execute an in-place upgrade while preserving user data.
content: hardware compatibility check, driver availability, TPM 2.0 for Win 11, disk space requirements
creating bootable USB (Rufus, Windows Media Creation Tool); ISO files; network PXE boot
choosing the installation partition; GPT vs. MBR; EFI System Partition; wiping vs. keeping data
language/region/keyboard, product key entry, custom vs. upgrade install, initial user setup
driver installation order, Windows Update, removing bloatware, joining domain or Azure AD
upgrade vs. clean install trade-offs; keeping files/apps/settings; rollback window (10 days)
assess: Lab: perform a clean Windows installation in a VM from ISO, complete post-install checklist
Upgrade scenario: list the steps to upgrade Windows 10 Home to Windows 11 Pro in-place
Short answer: why should drivers be installed before joining a domain after a clean install?

title: Installing Applications
obj: Students will be able to install, update, and remove applications on Windows, macOS, and Linux using multiple distribution methods.
content: MSI vs. EXE; silent install flags (/quiet, /norestart); Programs and Features; Winget
DMG drag-to-Applications; PKG installers; App Store; Homebrew for CLI tools
apt install, dnf install; dpkg/rpm for local packages; dependencies and conflicts
Group Policy software deployment, SCCM/Intune, self-service portals
32-bit vs. 64-bit; Windows compatibility mode; sandbox/container isolation
proper uninstall vs. just deleting; leftover registry entries; revo uninstaller approach
assess: Install lab: install, configure, and uninstall a specified application on Windows and Linux VMs
Silent install script: write a Winget or apt command to install three tools in one command
Short answer: what is the risk of downloading an installer from a random website instead of the vendor's official site?
